subject: Continuous Random Variables
question: Let X be a gamma distribution with parameters $(\alpha,\lambda)$. Let $Y = cX$ with $c > 0$. Show that $F_Y(y) = \frac{(\lambda/c)^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\int_0^y z^{\alpha-1}e^{-\lambda z/c}\,dz$. Hence, Y is a gamma distribution with parameters $\left(\alpha, \frac{\lambda}{c}\right)$.
answer: We have $F_Y(y) = \Pr(Y\le y) = \Pr\left(X\le \frac{y}{c}\right) = \frac{\lambda^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\int_0^{y/c} t^{\alpha-1}e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{(\lambda/c)^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\int_0^y z^{\alpha-1}e^{-\lambda z/c}\,dz$.
explanation: Answer: We have $F_Y(y) = \Pr(Y\le y) = \Pr\left(X\le \frac{y}{c}\right) = \frac{\lambda^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\int_0^{y/c} t^{\alpha-1}e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{(\lambda/c)^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\int_0^y z^{\alpha-1}e^{-\lambda z/c}\,dz$.
